title: Wet-felted Christmas stocking
subtitle: A sturdy hanging stocking in red and white merino
excerpt: A classic hanging stocking shape approximately 25 cm from cuff to toe, felted over a plastic-bag resist. Red merino body with a white cuff makes a durable seasonal piece. A good beginner project for learning the resist-felting technique.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from standard resist wet-felting methods taught in beginner wet-felting workshops. Plastic-bag resist technique for hollow three-dimensional forms is widely published in felting guides from the 1990s onward. No single public domain text.

glossaryTerms.definition: Fine-stapled wool from Merino sheep. Felts readily and produces a smooth, dense surface.
Wool fibre drawn into a long, loosely twisted ribbon, ready for spinning or felting.
An object or material placed within or around the wool before felting. It blocks or reduces fibre migration in the resisted area, creating pattern or texture.
Felting using warm water, soap, and agitation to cause wool fibres to interlock and shrink into a cohesive fabric.
The second stage of wet felting, where the pre-felted piece is agitated more vigorously to shrink and strengthen the fibres further.

content.text: resist stocking is made by layering
cut to the stocking shape. Once the wool is felted and the resist removed, the result is a hollow three-dimensional pocket. The technique is the same one used for felted bags and slippers: the
prevents the front and back layers from bonding together where the opening will be. At 25 cm this stocking is a generous size and will take around 2-3 hours of active work.

. Cuff: 30 g white merino roving. You will also need felting soap, hot water (60-65°C), a thick polythene bag for the resist, and a marker pen. Wool shrinks 25-30% when felted. Cut the resist template at 33 cm so the finished stocking reaches about 25 cm.

Draw a stocking shape on your plastic sheeting: 33 cm tall from cuff opening to toe, 15 cm wide at the cuff, with a rounded toe and a gentle heel curve. Cut out two identical shapes: one for the front and one for the back of the stocking. Alternatively, cut one piece and fold it in half lengthwise; the fold sits at the heel.

template on your bubble wrap. Lay red merino roving over it in two perpendicular layers, extending 4-5 cm beyond the resist edge all around; you will fold this overlap over later. Leave the top 8 cm of the cuff area in red but set aside enough white roving for the visible cuff layer. Wet thoroughly with hot soapy water and press firmly for 5-6 minutes until the roving begins to hold together.
Fold the overlap. Fold the 4-5 cm border of roving over onto the resist so it lies flat against the plastic. This forms the sealed edges of the stocking.

Second side. Carefully flip the whole piece over. Lay the second side: red roving in two perpendicular layers over the back of the resist, again extending 4-5 cm beyond the edge. For the top 8 cm of the cuff area on this side, lay white merino roving instead of red. Fold the border over and press as before.

Pre-felt both sides. Continue pressing and rubbing the surface through a cotton tea towel for 8-10 minutes until the roving holds together firmly and no longer shifts when you slide a hand under it. This is the pre-felt stage.

Roll and full. Roll the pre-felted stocking onto the bamboo mat and roll 30-40 times in each direction, turning the piece 90 degrees between cycles. Add hot water as needed. Continue for 20-25 minutes. The piece should shrink noticeably and feel quite firm. Alternate hot and cold water rinses during

Remove the resist. When the stocking feels firm and the surface does not stretch easily, cut or pull out the plastic resist through the cuff opening. The inside of the stocking will be slightly rough; this is normal. Reach inside and work your hand around to open up the toe and heel fully.

Final shaping. Continue fulling the stocking without the resist for a further 10 minutes to firm up the final shape. Push your hand inside the stocking periodically to shape the toe and heel from within. Rinse thoroughly in warm water, squeeze gently in a dry cotton tea towel, then stuff with crumpled newspaper or a plastic bag to hold the shape while it dries.

Hanging loop. Once dry, sew a short loop of cord, ribbon, or a length of twisted merino yarn through the top inner corner of the cuff. A loop approximately 8 cm long is enough to hang the stocking from a mantelpiece hook.

items.symptom: Front and back layers bonded together inside the stocking
items.cause: The resist was not wide enough, or the roving was laid too far over the edge before folding
items.fix: For a future piece, cut the resist at least 2 cm wider on each side. If the current piece has bonded, carefully snip the bond inside with sharp scissors and continue felting.

items.symptom: Stocking has a rough, uneven cuff edge
items.cause: The top edge was not neatened before drying
items.fix: Trim the cuff edge with sharp scissors while the stocking is slightly damp. The edge will felt in slightly as the piece dries.

items.symptom: White cuff shows red bleeding into it
items.cause: Red and white layers were laid too close together without a clear boundary
items.fix: On the next piece, leave a 2 cm gap between the red body roving and the start of the white cuff area, then blend the join deliberately with a thin mixed wisp.

content.text: The finished stocking is a sturdy piece of
and will not shrink further under normal washing. Hand wash in cool water if needed. Store stuffed with tissue paper to keep the shape between seasons. Keep away from moths:
is susceptible. A cedar block stored inside during summer storage is effective.
